questions.question: In ER modeling, what's the difference between an entity and an attribute?

options.text: An entity is a kind of thing you store data about (it becomes a table); an attribute is a single fact about that thing (it becomes a column).
options.explanation: Correct. The entity is the type — the box that becomes a table — and each attribute is one fact every instance has, which becomes a column.

options.text: An entity is a single row and an attribute is a single column.
options.explanation: An entity is the whole type, not one row. A specific row is called an instance (Michael Scott), while the entity (employee) is the shape every instance shares.

options.text: An entity is a number and an attribute is text.
options.explanation: The data type has nothing to do with the distinction. An attribute can be a number, text, or a date; what makes it an attribute is that it's a single fact about an entity.

options.text: An entity is a foreign key and an attribute is a primary key.
options.explanation: Keys are specific columns with special roles, not the entity/attribute distinction. An entity is a whole thing (a table); an attribute is any single fact (column) about it, key or not.

questions.question: An employee can work on many projects, and a project can have many employees. How do you store this many-to-many relationship?

options.text: Create an associative (junction) entity whose rows each record one employee–project pairing.
options.explanation: Correct. A many-to-many resolves into an associative entity — each row is one pairing, and it becomes the natural home for facts about the pairing itself, like the hours worked.

options.text: Put a list of project IDs in a single column on the employee table.
options.explanation: A column holds one value, not a list — which is exactly why the associative entity is needed. You can't cram many project IDs into one cell and still query it reliably.

options.text: Add a single foreign key to whichever table is more important.
options.explanation: A single foreign key can only capture one-to-many. It can't represent 'many' on both sides — that's the whole reason the associative entity exists.

options.text: Nothing special is needed — a plain relationship line handles it automatically.
options.explanation: A many-to-many line can't be built directly in a real database. You must resolve it into an associative entity before there's any table to store the pairings in.

questions.question: The `assignment` table's primary key is `(employee_id, project_id)`, both borrowed from its parents. Is its relationship to `employee` identifying or non-identifying?

options.text: Identifying — the parent's key is part of the child's own primary key, so the assignment can't be identified (or exist) without the parent.
options.explanation: Correct. Apply the one-question test: is the parent's key part of the child's primary key? Here yes, so it's identifying — and assignment is a weak entity that borrows its identity.

options.text: Non-identifying — the foreign key is just an ordinary attribute here.
options.explanation: It would be non-identifying only if employee_id were an ordinary column. But it's part of assignment's primary key, which makes the relationship identifying.

options.text: It depends on how many rows are currently in the table.
options.explanation: Identifying vs. non-identifying is fixed by the design — is the parent's key in the child's PK? — not by the data that happens to be in the table.

options.text: Neither — associative entities have no relationships, only columns.
options.explanation: An associative entity has two relationships, one to each parent, and is almost always identifying on both sides. That's precisely what makes it the resolution of a many-to-many.

questions.question: You split employee credentials into a separate `user_account` table with a one-to-one relationship to `employee`. What's the security benefit?

options.text: You can grant access to `employee` without exposing the credentials, keeping the most sensitive attributes in a separately lockable entity.
options.explanation: Correct. A one-to-one split isolates crown-jewel attributes so you can lock that one table down harder — a confidentiality win, straight out of the CIA triad.

options.text: It makes every query against the employee table run faster.
options.explanation: Performance isn't the point here — and a split can even add a join. The reason to split is isolating sensitive data for access control, not speed.
Table structure alone doesn't stop injection — that's about how queries are built from input (Module 11). Splitting the table only limits what a leak exposes.

options.text: It removes the need for a primary key on either table.
options.explanation: Both tables still need primary keys — in fact the one-to-one relationship is implemented by keys. Splitting changes what's exposed, not whether keys are required.
